---
title: Payments
description: Monetize your Stack Auth project with Stripe-backed subscriptions, one-time purchases, and usage tracking
---

Stack Payments connects your Stack Auth project to Stripe via a managed Connect account. Products, prices, and items are part of your Stack configuration, while Stripe handles payment collection and compliance.

## Prerequisites

- Set the Stripe credentials in your deployment: `STACK_STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`, `STACK_STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET`, and `NEXT_PUBLIC_STACK_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY`.
- Complete the dashboard onboarding in `Payments → Products` to create or attach the Stripe Connect account.
- Register the webhook endpoint `/api/latest/integrations/stripe/webhooks` inside Stripe using the same webhook secret.

After onboarding, the project can create checkout URLs and respond to Stripe events.

## Concepts

### Products

Products (`config.payments.products[productId]`) describe what a customer buys. Each product targets a customer type and supports:

- Multiple prices with different currencies and billing intervals, or `"include-by-default"` to grant it automatically.
- `stackable` quantities for add-on style purchases.
- `serverOnly` to hide plans from client-side SDK calls.
- `isAddOnTo` to require another product before purchase.
- Optional `freeTrial` lengths for subscriptions.

### Catalogs

Catalogs keep mutually exclusive plans together. Only one product in the same catalog can be active, so purchasing a new plan automatically replaces the existing one. Default products (with `"include-by-default"`) can represent your free tier inside the same catalog.

### Items

Items (`config.payments.items[itemId]`) store entitlements such as credits, seats, or feature flags. Products grant items through `includedItems`, which lets you configure the base quantity, whether it repeats, and when it expires. Manual adjustments are tracked as ledger entries.

## Checkout flow

1. Call the SDK or Admin API to create a checkout URL.
2. Customers land on Stack’s hosted purchase page where they can pick prices and quantities.
3. The page initializes Stripe Elements, creates Payment Intents or Subscriptions, and confirms payment. Successful payments trigger webhooks that persist one-time purchases or subscriptions and refresh item balances.

Client example:

```typescript
const user = useUser({ or: "redirect" });
const checkoutUrl = await user.createCheckoutUrl({ productId: "prod_pro_monthly" });
window.location.href = checkoutUrl;
```

Server example:

```typescript
const serverUser = await stackServerApp.getUserOrThrow("user_123");
const checkoutUrl = await serverUser.createCheckoutUrl({ productId: "prod_enterprise_annual" });
return NextResponse.redirect(checkoutUrl);
```

## Managing entitlements

- Fetch balances with `getItem`/`useItem` on users or teams.
- Adjust usage through `POST /payments/items/{customer_type}/{customer_id}/{item_id}/update-quantity`.
- Grant products directly with `POST /payments/products/{customer_type}/{customer_id}` (server auth required).

Stack recomputes item balances from subscriptions, one-time purchases, and manual ledger changes. Repeating grants honour the configured cadence and expiry rules.

## Test mode

- `payments.testMode` toggles the hosted checkout’s “Bypass with test mode” button so you can simulate purchases without Stripe.
- When test mode is off, all purchases go through Stripe; ensure your Stripe account has completed onboarding to accept live charges.

## Observability

Use the dashboard’s **Customers** tab to audit a single customer and adjust balances, and the **Transactions** tab for a chronological view of subscriptions, one-time purchases, and manual item changes.

These pieces let you model free tiers, per-seat pricing, or usage-based add-ons while keeping billing logic in Stack Auth and payments in Stripe.
